dominantFailure: Individual livelihood subsidies fail T1 fundamentally due to private-good delivery architecture; only employment-based commons schemes (MGNREGS-NRM) achieve SOC by converging livelihood and commons asset creation. Post-scheme asset stewardship is the critical missing piece — most commons assets created lack mandatory 10-year governance obligations.

structuralFindings.dominantProblem: Employment-Based Commons Creation as the SOC Success Model — MGNREGS-NRM is the sole scheme that converges employment generation with commons asset creation
structuralFindings.socException: MGNREGS-NRM (4/4) succeeds because employment generation and commons asset creation converge in a single scheme; the only Rural Development scheme achieving full SOC
crossCuttingPriorities: Scale MGNREGS-NRM to 60%+ of Rural Development public work: Reorient from non-NRM works to commons asset creation (watershed/pasture/forest); increase NRM allocation from current 40% to 60%+ (policy change, no budget increase)

Link Individual Livelihood Schemes to Commons Participation: SHGs (BIHAN), RSETI graduates, MKSP women farmers must participate in identified commons asset management as condition for scheme eligibility
Establish Commons Stewardship Framework: Post-scheme 10-year community stewardship groups for all MGNREGS-NRM assets; MGNREGS wages during maintenance as incentive
Redesign Rural Skill Training Toward Commons Governance: RSETI curriculum to include commons management competency; graduates placed in Water User Groups, Forest Committees, and Grazing Committees
contextualFactors: Individual livelihood subsidies (BIHAN, MKSP, RSETI) fail T1 fundamentally without commons integration — this is a design constraint, not an implementation gap
MGNREGS-NRM is the foundational platform for rural SOC — scaling its NRM allocation from 40% to 60% is the highest-leverage single intervention available
Chhattisgarh has 60%+ rainfed agriculture and extensive degraded commons (pasturelands, watershed areas) — strong demand for NRM-based employment that aligns with SOC requirements
BIHAN's 5+ million SHG women represent a ready organizational platform for commons governance if schemes are redesigned to link SHG activities to commons management

schemes.name: MGNREGS — NRM Component (Natural Resource Management)
schemes.objective: Create public rural employment through natural resource management works (water harvesting, pasture development, forest restoration, soil conservation) that build commons assets while providing guaranteed wage employment.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (commons NRM assets: watersheds/pastures/forests on public land); T1.2=1 (community commons asset creation); T1.3~0.65 (≥60% expenditure on commons NRM); T1.4=2 (non-excludable resource benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (village-level employment); T2.2~0.65 (SC/ST/women ≥65% of MGNREGS workers); T2.3=1 (Gram Sabha oversight of work selection mandated by Act)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (guaranteed wage income mandated by law: 100 days @ statutory minimum); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000+/HH/year (household wage + NRM asset productivity); T3.3=2 (employment wages + NRM asset-based livelihoods); T3.4=1 (institutional buyer: government wage payment via DBT)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (60% NRM budget earmarked for regeneration works); T4.2=1 (sustainability focus embedded in work selection criteria); T4.3=1 (annual work quality audit + asset sustainability assessment); T4.4=1 (wage withholding if quality standards not met); T4.5=1 (fund reduction for non-compliance)

upgradePathway: Increase NRM Allocation to 60%+: Reorient from non-NRM works (roads, buildings) to NRM asset creation; policy directive to Block Development Officers; no additional budget required
Asset Productivity Linkage: Track annual productivity of MGNREGS-created NRM assets (water harvested/grazing improved/forest cover); publish at GP level; use as selection criterion for next cycle
Women's Leadership: Mandate 50% women in MGNREGS-NRM work selection committees; link to BIHAN SHGs for community asset stewardship
Long-term Asset Stewardship: 10-year community management groups for each NRM asset; MGNREGS wages during maintenance activities; MGNREGS Act amendment to allow maintenance employment

schemes.name: MGNREGS — Base Employment Component
schemes.objective: Provide guaranteed rural employment (100 days/year minimum) at statutory minimum wage to all rural households, ensuring income security during agricultural off-seasons.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (public works on commons public land); T1.2=1 (community commons governance); T1.3~0.75 (≥70% to commons works); T1.4=2 (non-excludable employment access)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (village-level employment); T2.2~0.65 (SC/ST/women ≥65%); T2.3=1 (Gram Sabha oversight mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (guaranteed wage mandated by MGNREGS Act); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000+/HH/year (100 days @ minimum wage); T3.3=2 (direct wages + commons benefit); T3.4=1 (government wage payment)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (60% non-NRM works lack sustainability mandate; T4 fails for non-NRM portion); T4.2: no long-term maintenance mechanism for non-NRM assets; T4.3=0 (no community stewardship framework mandated); T4.4=0 (asset maintenance responsibility unclear post-completion)

upgradePathway: Increase NRM Allocation to 60%+: Policy directive to shift work selection toward NRM; non-NRM works (roads, buildings) reduced to <40% of MGNREGS budget
Mandatory Asset Stewardship: 10-year community stewardship groups for every completed work; group members receive MGNREGS wages for maintenance activities
Asset Livelihood Linkage: Connect each MGNREGS asset to a livelihood pathway (water harvesting → irrigation cooperative; pasture development → grazing committee; forest → JFM committee)
Monitoring Framework: Annual asset condition assessment by independent agency; assets rated green/amber/red; red assets trigger stewardship committee review

schemes.name: BIHAN (NRLM) — SHG Promotion & Livelihood Development
schemes.objective: Promote Self-Help Groups (SHGs) of rural women for savings, credit, and livelihood activities, enabling household income generation through group-based micro-enterprises.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual household livelihood — private enterprise); T1.2=0 (group-based credit without commons resource); T1.3~0.05 (≥90% on individual member benefits); T1.4=0 (enterprise benefits excludable to individual member)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (village-level SHGs); T2.2~0.70 (SC/ST women ≥70%); T2.3=1 (women's group governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (micro-enterprise income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000+/HH/year; T3.3=3 (dairy/vegetable/handicraft); T3.4=0 (market-dependent; no institutional offtake guarantee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no sustainability mandate for commons resource); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0 (SHG health depends on individual profitability); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Commons-Linked Livelihood Groups: SHG eligibility requires participation in managing an identified commons asset (village pond/pasture/community forest); SHG loan tied to commons management outcomes
Commons Asset Component: 20% of SHG micro-loan disbursement designated for commons asset maintenance (water body desilting, pasture seeding, community forestry)
Group Sustainability Obligation: Each SHG sets aside 5% of annual savings for O&M of adopted commons asset; commons health tracked alongside SHG health
Monitoring & Enforcement: Annual commons asset health assessment by BDO; SHG rating includes commons management score; affects loan renewal and interest rate

schemes.name: Saghan Vanikaran (Community Afforestation)
schemes.objective: Implement community-based afforestation on village commons and degraded lands, creating employment through plantation work while building forest commons assets.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (community forest commons on village/revenue land); T1.2=1 (village afforestation on commons land); T1.3~0.80 (≥75% on commons plantation); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (village-level beneficiaries); T2.2~0.55 (SC/ST+women ≥55%); T2.3=1 (community forest committee governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (plantation employment + future forest income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.7,000+/HH/year (employment ₹3,000 + forest income ₹4,000 post-maturation); T3.3=3 (employment/NTFP/fuelwood/timber); T3.4=1 (institutional buyers: Forest Department)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no tree survival monitoring post-plantation; 30-40% sapling mortality unmonitored); T4.2: no maintenance obligation; T4.3=0 (no post-project community stewardship); T4.4=0 (no replenishment obligation)

upgradePathway: Tree Survival Monitoring: Quarterly survival census (year 1-3); payment withheld if survival <80%; replacement planting mandatory at plantation committee's expense
Community Stewardship Fund: 10% of plantation capital deposited to 10-year maintenance fund before project handover; community forest committee manages disbursements
Benefit-Sharing Agreement: Pre-plantation formalization of income-sharing formula among participating households; lodged with Gram Panchayat before work commences
Continuous Afforestation Obligation: Forest committee must plant replacement trees ≥100% of any harvested trees; carry-forward obligation linked to future grant eligibility

schemes.name: Sansad Adarsh Gram Yojana (Model Village Development)
schemes.objective: Develop select villages as model settlements through integrated infrastructure, livelihood support, and governance improvements, demonstrating sustainable village development.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C/A (mixed: individual housing + community infrastructure; commons component <50%); T1.2=0 (no specific commons focus mandated); T1.3~0.40; T1.4=0 (mixed benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70; T2.2: no clear SC/ST priority; T2.3=0 (traditional panchayat, not commons-focused governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (infrastructure output, not livelihood income mandate); Delta-Y: 0 (no income pathway mandated); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0; T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0 (no sustainability framework); T4.4=0; T4.5=0 (post-project infrastructure orphaned)

upgradePathway: Note: This is an integrated development program falling primarily outside SOC scope.
SOC Convergence: Reserve 50% of Adarsh Gram funds for commons-specific investments (water/forest/pasture); link to FES SOC framework metrics
Commons Governance Pilot: Use Adarsh Gram as commons governance innovation lab; test SOC upgrade mechanisms before scaling
Performance Metrics: Add commons health indicators to Adarsh Gram success criteria alongside infrastructure metrics

schemes.name: RSETI (Rural Skill Development & Entrepreneurship)
schemes.objective: Provide vocational training and entrepreneurship support to rural youth, enabling livelihood diversification through skilled employment or micro-enterprise establishment.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual skill/knowledge — private good); T1.2=0 (individual training model; no commons resource); T1.3~0.05 (expenditure on individual beneficiaries); T1.4=0 (skill benefit excludable to trained individual)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (village-level training centers); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST youth prioritized); T2.3=1 (training committee governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (skilled employment income mandated as training outcome); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000+/HH/year (skilled employment); T3.3=3 (construction/hospitality/retail); T3.4=0 (market-dependent; no institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no sustainability mandate); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0 (no post-training support or monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Commons Management Competency: Add mandatory commons governance module (water management, forest committees, cooperative governance) to all RSETI curricula
Placement in Commons Institutions: 30% of RSETI placement target in commons institutions (Water User Groups, JFM committees, MFP Federation); government placement guarantee
Livelihood-Commons Linkage: RSETI graduates managing commons earn supplementary income from commons revenue; creates incentive for skilled commons governance
Long-term Career Pathway: Commons Management as formal career track (Block Commons Manager positions); RSETI as training pipeline for government commons governance roles

schemes.name: MKSP (Millets Cluster Development)
schemes.objective: Support women farmers in millet cultivation through inputs, training, and market linkage, promoting nutritious crop production and women farmer empowerment.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual private farm); T1.2=0 (private farmer inputs on private land); T1.3~0.05 (≥90% to individual farmer inputs); T1.4=0 (benefit excludable to individual farmer)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (village-level millet cluster); T2.2~0.80 (women farmers ≥80%); T2.3=1 (women's farmer groups governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (millet income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000+/HH/year (millet premium over cereals); T3.3=2 (nutrition + income pathway); T3.4=0 (market-dependent; no institutional procurement guarantee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no soil health mandate; no rotation obligation); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0 (no collective marketing sustainability); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Commons Land Component: Designate 20% of cluster area on village commons/fallow land; jointly managed by women farmer collective; commons millet income distributed cooperatively
Soil Health Mandate: Annual organic matter investment (composting/green manuring) as subsidy continuation condition; soil health tested every 3 years
Collective Marketing Sustainability: Producer cooperative mandatory for all MKSP clusters; TRIFED or state procurement agreement for millet offtake; price floor guaranteed
Water Conservation Obligation: Each MKSP cluster maintains water conservation structures (farm ponds/bunds) covering ≥10% of cluster area; link to JJM source sustainability

schemes.name: PM Awas Yojana — Gramin (Rural Housing)
schemes.objective: Provide financial assistance for construction of pucca (permanent) houses for rural poor households, improving living standards and housing security.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (individual housing on private plot — private asset); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.02 (≥95% to individual house construction); T1.4=0 (highly excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (village-level targeting); T2.2~0.70 (SC/ST ≥70% beneficiaries; BPL priority); T2.3=1 (community verification process through Gram Sabha)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (housing asset, not livelihood income); Delta-Y: 0 (no income pathway mandated); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Note: This is an individual housing subsidy scheme — falls outside direct SOC scope.
SOC Convergence: Link PMAY-G to commons participation; condition housing on beneficiary joining Water User Group or village commons committee
Community Infrastructure Overlay: Dedicate 5% of PMAY-G budget to shared sanitation/water commons for beneficiary cluster
Skill Linkage: PMAY-G construction as RSETI training site; beneficiary family members trained in construction; commons management skills bundled in training
